one_line_takeaway: CoCA shows aligned MLLMs still 'know' malicious images are unsafe — the signal is just muffled by the modality gap — and recovers it at inference time by contrastively amplifying the logit gap a safety principle induces, cutting attack success rates substantially and beating SFT on utility, with no training required.
key_finding: CoCA substantially lowers ASR across attack types and models while preserving utility, and does so better than both a plain safety system prompt and SFT-based safety tuning. On LLaVA-7B TYPO images, Illegal-Activity ASR drops 52.5 -> 36.0 (system prompt) -> 17.5 (CoCA); Malware 68.2 -> 27.3; Pornography 72.5 -> 40.3. On the harder FigStep benchmark, average ASR falls from 76.0 to 42.7. Gains grow with scale: LLaVA-13B TYPO Illegal-Activity drops from 36.1 to 4.1 with CoCA, attributed to larger models yielding a stronger safety 'contrast vector'. Utility is essentially intact (GQA 61.9->61.3, VQA 78.5->78.3, MMvet avg 31.6->32.8, even slightly up) and on MME CoCA (1704.8) beats VLGuard SFT (1679.4) while being training-free.
core_contribution: The paper (CoCA, COLM 2024) makes two contributions of its own. (1) An empirical observation: multimodal LLMs (MLLMs) retain the safety-awareness their underlying text LLM acquired during SFT/RLHF, but this awareness is weakened by the image-text 'modality gap'. Simply appending a safety principle (e.g. 'The assistant cannot provide answers related to illegal activities') to the input measurably lowers the attack success rate on malicious image queries, proving the latent safety-awareness is transferable to image inputs. (2) A training-free inference-time defense, Constitutional Calibration (CoCA), which amplifies the effect of that safety principle via contrastive decoding: at each decoding step it computes the logit difference between the model's prediction WITH and WITHOUT the safety principle S ('safety delta'), scales it by a factor alpha, and adds it back to the original logits before softmax. This regains safety-awareness without fine-tuning and without degrading the model's visual understanding/reasoning. Note: contrastive decoding itself is cited prior work (Li et al. 2023c and others in Related Work) applied to LLMs/translation; the paper's novelty is being first to apply contrast on the SAME model, keyed on a safety principle, to MLLM safety.
threat_model: Jailbreaking of aligned MLLMs through malicious VISUAL inputs. The attacker supplies an image that carries harmful intent, paired with an innocuous-looking textual query (e.g. an image of a bomb + 'how to make this item?'). Because the text-only LLM was safety-aligned but the vision modality was bolted on later with far less alignment data, the modality gap lets harmful images bypass the model's safety training and elicit harmful responses. Three concrete image-based attack forms from MM-SafetyBench are targeted: Stable-Diffusion-generated images (SD) directly depicting the query, Typography images (TYPO, harmful text rendered as an image / OCR-style), and combined SD+TYPO; plus FigStep, which converts harmful instructions into typographic images. CoCA is an inference-time DEFENSE, not an attack.
limitations: (1) The defense hinges on principle wording: an overly detailed principle (P4) can BACKFIRE — CoCA with P4 on Illegal-Activity raises ASR to 54.6, WORSE than the undefended 52.5 baseline — because a complex principle produces a confusing delta; the authors conclude concise, task-specific principles are essential, which means the method needs per-scenario prompt tuning. (2) SD+TYPO (the strongest combined attack) is only partially mitigated: LLaVA-7B Illegal-Activity SD+TYPO falls just 71.1 -> 62.8, far less than the TYPO-only case. (3) Requires two forward passes per decoding step (with/without safety prompt), roughly doubling inference cost, and needs alpha tuning. (4) Residual ASR remains non-trivial (e.g. FigStep still 42.7 avg after defense). (5) ASR is scored by a GPT-4 judge, inheriting that judge's noise/bias. (6) Evaluated only on open-source MLLMs and the MM-SafetyBench/FigStep threat distribution; adaptive attackers aware of CoCA are not studied.

datasets_used: MM-SafetyBench (SD, TYPO, SD+TYPO image splits; categories: Illegal Activity, Hate Speech, Malware, Physical Harm, Fraud, Pornography)
FigStep (500 harmful questions rendered as typographic images)
VLGuard (used as the SFT safety-data baseline, Zong et al. 2024a)
quantitative_results: LLaVA-7B TYPO Illegal Activity ASR: 52.5 baseline -> 36.0 system prompt -> 17.5 CoCA
LLaVA-7B TYPO Malware ASR: 68.2 -> 27.3 CoCA; Pornography 72.5 -> 40.3 CoCA
LLaVA-7B SD+TYPO Illegal Activity ASR: 71.1 -> 62.8 CoCA (weakest mitigation)
FigStep LLaVA-7B average ASR: 76.0 -> 42.7 CoCA (Illegal Activity 80.0->50.0)
LLaVA-13B TYPO Illegal Activity ASR: 36.1 -> 4.1 CoCA (gains grow with scale)
Principle backfire: CoCA with detailed P4 on Illegal Activity = 54.6 ASR, worse than 52.5 baseline; best is task-specific concise P2/P3 (17.5/18.5)
Utility preserved: GQA 61.9->61.3, VQA 78.5->78.3; MMvet average 31.6->32.8; MME total 1726.9 (LLaVA-7B) vs 1679.4 (VLGuard SFT) vs 1704.8 (CoCA)
Main hyper-parameter alpha=4; larger alpha yields better safety (Figure 3)
baselines_compared: Safety principle placed in the system prompt (no logit calibration)
SFT-based safety tuning on VLGuard data (LLaVA-1.5 + SFT), compared on MME utility
Different principle designs P1-P4 (generic vs task-specific vs detailed illustration)
